metadata.career_relevance: Sliding Window Maximum (LeetCode #239) is the definitive O(n) deque problem — asked at Amazon, Google, and Flipkart. The pattern combines sliding window (M30) with monotonic stack (T2) into a deque. Once mastered, it unlocks a family of 'maximum in range' problems.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Flipkart analytics: find max order value in every rolling 3-day window
Orders: [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], k=3
Naive O(nk): check all k elements each window → O(n×3) = TLE
Deque O(n): maintain decreasing deque of indices

Windows:  [1,3,-1] [3,-1,-3] [-1,-3,5] [-3,5,3] [5,3,6] [3,6,7]
Maxes:    3        3         5         5        6       7
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Sliding Window Maximum O(n), Jump Game, and related deque problems.

visual_aid.description: Deque trace for [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7] k=3. Show deque contents (indices with values) at each step. i=0: deque=[0(1)]. i=1: 3>1 → remove 0, add 1 → deque=[1(3)]. i=2: -1<3 → add 2 → deque=[1(3),2(-1)]. Window full: result[0]=nums[1]=3. i=3: -3<-1 → add 3 → deque=[1(3),2(-1),3(-3)]. result[1]=nums[1]=3. i=4: 5>all → clear deque, add 4 → deque=[4(5)]. result[2]=5. Continue...
visual_aid.alt_text: Monotonic deque for sliding window maximum

worked_example.scenario: Jump Game II (LeetCode #45) — minimum jumps to end.
worked_example.problem_statement: nums=[2,3,1,1,4], reach end in minimum jumps. Answer = 2.

step_by_step_solution.details: Track current reach and max reach. When we reach the end of current jump range, increment jumps, update reach to max reach seen.

things_to_notice: Greedy: always jump to extend farthest reach
currEnd: boundary of current jump range
When i==currEnd: must jump, extend to farthest
O(n) time, O(1) space — no deque needed here, but similar range-tracking idea

industry_examples.use_case: Rolling max order value for dashboard. O(n) deque vs O(nk) naive.

applications.interview_relevance: Sliding Window Max: definitive deque problem. 'Max in each window' → always think deque first. O(n) vs O(nk) naive is the key insight.
applications.real_world_workflow: Deque decision: need both ends? → Deque. Sliding window max/min → monotonic deque. Queue/Stack combined → Deque.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Forgetting to remove indices that have left the window. If deque.peekFirst() <= i-k, that index is no longer in the window [i-k+1, i] and must be removed from front.
common_gaps.remediation: Always step 1: check if front of deque is out of window. Condition: deque.peekFirst() < i-k+1, equivalently deque.peekFirst() <= i-k. Remove from front (pollFirst). This must happen BEFORE recording result.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Sliding window max returns stale values from previous windows

key_takeaways: Deque: O(1) at both ends — addFirst/addLast, removeFirst/removeLast
Sliding window max: monotonic decreasing deque of INDICES
Step 1: remove out-of-window index from front
Step 2: remove smaller values from back (they can never be max)
Step 3: record result when window full (i >= k-1)
O(n): each element added/removed at most once
Deque = double-ended queue: combines stack (one end) and queue (other end)

self_check_questions.question: Why does the sliding window maximum deque remove elements from the BACK when a larger element arrives?
self_check_questions.answer: Because those smaller elements can never become the maximum. Suppose we have indices [..., j, i] in the deque where j < i (j came before i), and nums[j] < nums[i]. As the window slides right, whenever j is in the window, i is also in the window (since i > j and they're both in the current window). Since nums[i] > nums[j], j will never be the maximum of any future window — it's always dominated by i. So we can safely discard j. This maintains the deque in monotonically decreasing order: the front is always the maximum of the current window.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Stack & Queue Synthesis — implement LRU Cache, calculator, task scheduler.
